topic: Applied Psychology Options (Child / Criminal / Environmental / Sport and Exercise)
subject: psychology

items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.

items.q: Top-down offender profiling was developed by:
items.why: It's FBI. “Canter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Differential association theory was proposed by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sutherland. “Eysenck” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Lombroso publish his atavistic form theory?
items.why: The real figure is 1876 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
